text.title: SLO Definition Per Class — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every SLO definition must satisfy. The rules turn SLO authoring from dashboard guesswork into a defensible engineering artefact.

rule.statement: The first decision when defining an SLO is the service's CLASS, not its metrics. Classes are from the closed enum: API, async-worker, batch, static-asset, scheduled-job, gateway, ML-inference. Custom classes require explicit team approval.

rationale.source: Google SRE Workbook chapter on choosing SLIs; field experience

rule.rationale: Starting from metrics produces SLOs that fit the existing dashboard, not the user expectation. Starting from class invokes a template that has been validated against the failure modes of that class. The class drives the SLI selection.

rule.statement: Each service uses the SLIs from its class template AND no more than 2 additional service-specific SLIs. SLOs MUST be computable from the chosen SLIs.
rule.rationale: More than ~4 SLIs per service produces dashboards nobody reads and alerts nobody responds to. The class template is the minimum; the additional 2 cap is the limit. Beyond that, the team is measuring everything and prioritising nothing.

rule.statement: SLO targets MUST be set by the product or business owner answering: "what user-experienced error rate is acceptable?" Targets derived from current performance ("we are already at 99.7, set the SLO at 99.7") are forbidden.

rationale.source: Google SRE Workbook chapter on user-centric SLOs; product-engineering alignment literature

rule.rationale: Current-performance-derived targets create a system that defends mediocrity. User-acceptable targets create a system that ships when the user is happy. They are sometimes the same number; the difference is the conversation.

rule.statement: Every SLO has an accompanying one-page budget policy document: what triggers a freeze, what reverts it, who owns it. Without the doc, the SLO has no operational meaning.

rationale.source: Google SRE Workbook; field experience with policy-less budgets

rule.rationale: An error budget without a policy is just a metric. The policy turns the budget into a contract between engineering, product, and operations: when budget burns, named consequences follow.

rule.statement: Default SLO window: 30-day rolling. Shorter (7-day) requires explicit justification (typically high-volume APIs); longer (90-day) is allowed for batch / scheduled with documented reason. Calendar-month windows are forbidden.
rule.rationale: 7-day windows are noisy; 90+ day windows hide drift. 30-day is the canonical balance. Calendar-month windows create end-of-month panic when budget exhausts and a January-1 reset that masks systemic issues.

list.item: API: request_success_rate (2xx + 3xx as success), request_latency_p99, request_latency_p50. Target template: 99.9% success / p99 ≤ N ms.
async-worker: processing_success_rate, queue_age_p99, retry_rate. Target: 99.5% success / queue-age p99 ≤ N min.
batch: completion_on_time_rate, completeness_rate (all expected outputs produced). Target: 99% on-time / 100% completeness.
static-asset: delivery_success_rate, freshness_lag_p99. Target: 99.95% delivery / freshness ≤ 5 min.
scheduled-job: execution_success_rate, on_time_start_rate. Target: 99% success / 99% on-time.
gateway: same as API plus auth_success_rate, downstream_5xx_rate-pass-through. Target: 99.95% / p99 ≤ N ms.
ML-inference: inference_success_rate, latency_p99, quality_metric (judge or proxy). Target: 99% / p99 ≤ N ms / quality ≥ baseline.

example.title: API SLO derived from product conversation
example.code: service: checkout-api class: api sli_set: - request_success_rate # template - request_latency_p99 # template - request_latency_p50 # template - payment_attempt_success_rate # service-specific (1 of 2 allowed extras) slo: request_success_rate: target: 99.95 # product set: each 1% maps to ~$10k revenue lost window: 30d rolling request_latency_p99: target: <= 800ms # product set: above 1s, cart abandonment +30% window: 30d rolling budget_policy: docs/policies/checkout-api-budget.md
example.note: Illustrates r1 (class first), r2 (template + 1 extra), r3 (product set targets with rationale), r4 (policy doc), r5 (30-day window).

example.title: Dashboard-driven SLO
example.code: service: checkout-api sli_set: - cpu_usage # not user-facing - mem_usage # not user-facing slo: cpu_usage: target: < 80% window: calendar-month budget_policy: (none)
example.note: r1 violated (no class), r3 violated (no product input), r5 violated (calendar-month), r4 violated (no policy). CPU SLO does not map to user pain.
